name: Wellens Syndrome
identifyBy: Type A: Biphasic T waves in V2–V3 (initial positive deflection then negative)
Type B: Deep symmetric T-wave inversion in V2–V3 (more common, 75%)
Minimal or no ST elevation - patient may be pain-free when ECG taken
Preserved R wave progression (no Q waves - myocardium not yet infarcted)
History: recent chest pain, now resolved or minimal

measurements.stSegment: Minimal or no elevation; characteristic T-wave changes

management: Urgent evacuation even if pain-free - risk of imminent LAD occlusion and anterior STEMI
Do NOT stress test - may precipitate complete LAD occlusion
Urgent cardiology consultation and coronary angiography
pearls: Wellens syndrome indicates critical (>90%) proximal LAD stenosis - without intervention, anterior STEMI is imminent
The pain-free Wellens patient is deceptively stable - they are at extreme risk
Stress testing is CONTRAINDICATED - it can trigger complete occlusion
Type A (biphasic) can be subtle - measure T wave carefully in V2–V3
Wellens is a reperfusion pattern - it occurs when the vessel has transiently opened then re-narrowed
differentials: RV strain (T inversion V1–V4 but different context)

qaNotes: Ischaemia/STEMI morphology retained from source content; verify affected leads in multi-lead view.
